coredata.dc:title: Nafion-stabilized black phosphorus nanosheets-maltosyl-β-cyclodextrin as a chiral sensor for tryptophan enantiomers
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Jiao
Yu, Jin-Gang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we have fabricated nafion (NF) stabilized black phosphorus nanosheets (BPNSs) and 6-O-α-maltosyl-β-cyclodextrin (G2-β-CD) composite (BPNSs-G2-β-CD) as novel electrochemical sensoring platform for chiral recognition of tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers. BPNSs-G2-β-CD composite modified glassy carbon electrode (BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE) was further coated with NF which served as a protective film to immobilize BPNSs-G2-β-CD on the electrode surface to achieve high stability. Under the optimum conditions, the oxidation peak current ratio of L-Trp to D-Trp (IL/ID) and the difference between the peak potential (ΔEp = ED - EL) were observed to be 1.49 and 20 mV at NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE by square wave voltammetry (SWV). In addition, a linear calibration curve could be obtained for peak current versus Trp enantiomers in the concentration range 0.01–1.00 mM with detection limits of 1.07 μM and 1.71 μM for L-Trp and D-Trp (signal-to-noise ratio of 3, S/N = 3), respectively. The chiral recognition mechanism was also proposed, and the intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions as well as the hydrophobic-cavity-triggered embedding effect dominated the effective chiral recognition. Moreover, the proposed NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE showed excellent stability, good reproducibility and anti-interference capability. Therefore, the designed chiral sensor is expected to be practically applied for the sensitive recognition of Trp enantiomers in real samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the samples: (A) (B) HR-TEM images of BPNSs; FE-SEM images of (C) BPNSs/GCE, (D) BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE, (E) NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE, (F) G2-β-CD and (G) BPNSs-G2-β-CD composite; Corresponding C (I), O (J) and P (K) elemental mapping of (H) BPNSs-G2-β-CD composite; (L) EDS analysis of BPNSs-G2-β-CD composite.

Electrochemical behaviors of the electrodes: (A) CV of modified electrodes in 1.0 mM of K3Fe(CN)6 solution (containing 0.1 M of KCl) at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1; (B) Nyquist plots of the electrodes in 1.0 mM K3Fe(CN)6 solution (containing 0.1 M KCl) at open-circuit potential conditions (AC frequency range: 1 Hz to 1000 kHz; AC amplitude: 5.0 mV), and the equivalent circuit (inset).
SWV curves of 1.0 mM (a) L-Trp and (b) D-Trp at the electrodes: (A) bare GCE; (B) BPNSs/GCE; (C) G2-β-CD/GCE; (D) BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE; (E) NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE.

Effects of experimental conditions on the recognition efficiency of Trp enantiomers (1.0 mM) at NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE: (A) Effects of buffer electrolytes (BR: 0.04 M, pH 7.0; PB: 0.1 M, pH 7.0; PBS: 0.01 M, pH 7.0; HAc-NaAc: 0.1 M, pH 7.0); (B) Effects of pH value (5, 6, 7, 8 and 9); (C) The variation of potential versus pH for L-Trp and D-Trp at NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE; (D) Effects of temperature (10 °C, 15 °C, 20 °C, 25 °C, 30 °C, 35 °C and 40 °C). Error bars represent the standard deviations for three independent measurements.
The calibration plots of peak current versus Trp concentration at NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE. (Insets: SWVs of (a) L-Trp and (b) D-Trp with different concentrations, respectively).

(A) Recognition efficiency of NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE toward Tyr, Trp, His and Phe. Error bars represent standard deviation for three independent measurements; (B) Chemical structures of Tyr, Trp, His and Phe.

The amperometric response current ratios for 0.1 mM Trp enantiomers in the existence of 1.0 mM of various potential interfering agents (ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glc), l-serine (L-Ser), L-phenylalanine (L-Phe), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), copper chloride (CuCl2), cmagnesium nitrate [Mg(NO3)2], aluminum nitrate [Al(NO3)3] or zinc nitrate [Zn(NO3)2]) at NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE in 0.1 M PB (pH = 7.0): (A) L-Trp and (B) D-Trp.

(A) Schematic representation of the workflow for the liquid-phase exfoliation of BPNSs [inset: (a) top and side views of the atomic structure of BP; (b) Top view of an individual BP sheet (consisting of two atomic layers that are overlapped)]; (B) Schematic diagrams of the experimental setup: the fabrication of NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE and its application in electrochemical recognition of Trp enantiomers.
A proposed mechanism for the chiral electrochemical recognition of Trp enantiomers at NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE.
A comparison of the developed sensors in the chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: SCS: sulfonated CS; DPV: differential pulse voltammograms; HP-β-CD: hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin; P-L-Glu: poly(L-glutamic acid); CMC: carboxymethyl cellulose; PDA: polydopamine; EA: ethylenediamine; NCCS: N-carboxymethyl chitosan; GNs: graphene nanosheets.

head.ce:title: Nafion-stabilized black phosphorus nanosheets-maltosyl-β-cyclodextrin as a chiral sensor for tryptophan enantiomers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410083, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we have fabricated nafion (NF) stabilized black phosphorus nanosheets (BPNSs) and 6-O-α-maltosyl-β-cyclodextrin (G2-β-CD) composite (BPNSs-G2-β-CD) as novel electrochemical sensoring platform for chiral recognition of tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers. BPNSs-G2-β-CD composite modified glassy carbon electrode (BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE) was further coated with NF which served as a protective film to immobilize BPNSs-G2-β-CD on the electrode surface to achieve high stability. Under the optimum conditions, the oxidation peak current ratio of L-Trp to D-Trp (IL/ID) and the difference between the peak potential (ΔEp = ED - EL) were observed to be 1.49 and 20 mV at NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE by square wave voltammetry (SWV). In addition, a linear calibration curve could be obtained for peak current versus Trp enantiomers in the concentration range 0.01–1.00 mM with detection limits of 1.07 μM and 1.71 μM for L-Trp and D-Trp (signal-to-noise ratio of 3, S/N = 3), respectively. The chiral recognition mechanism was also proposed, and the intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions as well as the hydrophobic-cavity-triggered embedding effect dominated the effective chiral recognition. Moreover, the proposed NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE showed excellent stability, good reproducibility and anti-interference capability. Therefore, the designed chiral sensor is expected to be practically applied for the sensitive recognition of Trp enantiomers in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality is one of the significant characteristics of nature [1]. As the basic chemical property of the living world, it affects the life processes [2]. Enantioselective recognition of many biologically active chiral molecules, especially amino acids, is very important in living systems [3], because amino acid molecules with different configurations usually have identical physical/chemical properties and completely different physiological and pharmacological activities, and the two enantiomers even play opposite roles in the living systems [4]. For example, one isomer of tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers, L-Trp, is an essential component of protein and a precursor of the neurotransmitter serotonin [5], and the imbalance or lack of L-Trp may cause several chronic diseases [6]. However, D-Trp is not an essential amino acid and cannot be directly utilized by organisms [7]. Therefore, it is of great significance to discriminate the two enantiomers in chemical and biological systems.

Various analytical techniques have been widely applied for the stereoselective recognition of the enantiomers of Trp, such as colorimetry [7], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8], fluorescence spectroscopy [9] and molecular imprinting techniques [10]. However, most of the above techniques require complex instruments and tedious sample pretreatment, therefore, they are time-consuming and costly. Electrochemical chiral sensing has received extensively attention in the recognition and determination of Trp enantiomers [11,12] due to its convenient operation, low cost, high sensitivity and easy miniaturization [13,14].

Chiral enantiomers are widely found in food, biology and the environments. Therefore, electrochemical detection methods are also important in food [15], biological [16–24] and environmental analysis [25]. To improve the electrochemical responses of the sensor, various types of materials including poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC) membranes [26–37], crown ether [38], calixarenes [39,40], fullerene-C60 [41–43], polypyrrole [44], and metal oxide (ZnO [45] and so on) were used as efficient modifiers. The electrochemical properties such as sensitivity and specificity are greatly improved. Similarly, for electrochemical chiral recognition, the selection of chiral recognition materials or chiral selectors is particularly important [46]. More and more natural polysaccharides including potato starch (PS) [47], soluble starch (SS) [48], chitosan (CS) [48–50], cyclodextrins (CDs) (α-CD [51], β-CD [52] and γ-CD [46]) and their derivatives [14] have been widely served as chiral selectors, which are relatively inexpensive, extensive, nontoxic, biocompatible and biodegradable [53]. Particularly, β-CD, a cyclic polysaccharide composed of seven glucose molecules with a hydrophobic inner cavity and a hydrophilic exterior, has been used more widely [54]. The special molecular structure of β-CD could from host-guest inclusion complexes with various organic molecules through geometric matching modes of the inside cavities and the branched hydroxyl groups. In addition, the wide application of β-CD in electrochemical chiral sensing is due to its high molecular selectivity and enantioselectivity [55,56]. Compared with β-CD, its derivatives exhibited better chiral recognition ability due to their more active interaction sites for the analytes [57]. Thus, β-CD derivatives have been used more extensively for electrochemical chiral sensing. For example, three-dimensional-graphene/hydroxypropyl-β-CD (3D-G/HP-β-CD) was used as a chiral sensing platform for electrochemical recognition of Trp enantiomers [14].

Nanomaterials, including multi-walled carbon nanotube (MWCNTs) [58–60], single-walled CNTs (SWCNTs) [61,62], graphene oxide (GO) [63,64], reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [13], graphene nanosheets (GNSs) [65], metal nanoparticles (PtNPs [66], AuNPs [67,68], AgNPs [69]) have attracted more and more attention from researchers as novel platforms for sensing. Black phosphorus (BP), also known as phosphorene, is highly similar to graphite. BP is composed of a single element, which can be easily exfoliated into single/few layered structures [70,71]. BP exhibits some unique semiconducting characteristics, strong anisotropic transport, excellent electron and hole mobility, adjustable particle sizes, low defect density and ultra-high specific surface area [72,73]. Therefore, BP has attracted great interest for a wide range of applications such as energy storage, optoelectronics, catalysis, biomedicine and sensors. For example, a non-enzymatic hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) sensor was constructed based on supercritical carbon dioxide assisted rapid synthesis of few-layered BP nanosheets (BPNSs) [74]. The functionalization of BPNSs with poly-l-lysine (PLL) could facilitate the binding of generated anti-myoglobin DNA aptamers on nanostructured electrodes for label-free detection of myoglobin [75].

Nafion (NF), a copolymer of polytetrafluoroethylene and perfluoro-3,6-diepoxy-4-methyl-7-decene-sulfuric acid, is a perfluorosulfonated cation exchanger. NF has been widely used to stabilize electrochemical sensors due to its antifouling ability, chemical inertness, hydrophilicity and chemical stability [76,77]. Herein, a novel, stable and stereoselective electrochemical sensor based on NF/BPNSs-6-O-α-maltosyl-β-cyclodextrin modified glassy carbon electrode (NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE) was firstly proposed and used to recognize Trp enantiomers (Scheme 1 ). BPNSs-G2-β-CD composite was drop-coated onto GCE (BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE), followed by coating with NF which served as the protective film to immobilize BPNSs-G2-β-CD on the electrode surface. The proposed NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE showed high stability, high resistance to interference and good repeatability as well as excellent reproducibility. The proposed chiral sensor would provide useful references for constructing novel chiral platforms.

Black phosphorus (BP) crystals were bought from NanJing Ji Cang Nano Technology Co., Ltd. (NanJing, China). Nafion (NF, 5 wt%) was purchased from Du Pont. Co. Ltd. (USA). D-tryptophan (D-Trp, purity of 98 wt%), L-Trp (purity of 99 wt%), D-tyrosine (D-Tyr, purity of 98 wt%), L-Tyr (purity of 99 wt%), D-phenylalanine (D-Phe, purity of 98 wt%), L-Phe (purity of 99 wt%), D-histidine (D-His, purity of 99 wt%), L-His (purity of 99 wt%), 6-O-α-maltosyl-β-cyclodextrin hydrate (G2-β-CD, purity of 98 wt%) and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Potassium ferricyanide [K3Fe(CN)6] and potassium chloride (KCl) were provided by Tianjin Broadcom Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4 12H2O), sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate (NaH2PO4 2H2O), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), concentrated nitric acid (HNO3), boric acid (H3BO3), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), acetic acid (HAc) and sodium acetate (NaAc) were obtained from bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Alumina polishing powder (0.05 μm) was purchased from Lab Testing Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 were dissolved in double distilled water (DDW) to prepare 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PB, pH = 7.0). Electrochemical experiments were carried out in PB (0.1 M) solution. The required concentrations of D-Trp and L-Trp (1.0 mM) were prepared by diluting the stock solution with PB. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification, and DDW was used throughout the experiments.

The as-prepared BPNSs were observed on high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL Ltd.; Tokyo, Japan). The morphologies of BPNSs, BPNSs-G2-β-CD composite and NF modified electrode were characterized by a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, TESCAN MIRA3 LMH/LMU; Czech), and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis was carried out on the same instrument to study the chemical composition of the samples, and elemental mapping was used to evaluate the elemental distribution of carbon (C), oxygen (O) and phosphorus (P). All electrochemical experiments were measured on a CHI660E Electrochemical Workstation (Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd.; Shanghai, China). A conventional three-electrode system was used in which an Ag/AgCl electrode, platinum wire electrode, and bare GCE or NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE (diameter: 3.0 mm) were adopted as the reference electrode, counter electrode and working electrode, respectively. All electrochemical experiments were performed at room temperature (25 °C) except for temperature control experiments. Error bars represented the standard deviation of three independent measurements.

BPNSs were prepared by an exfoliation of BP crystals as previously reported [71,73]. Briefly, 5.0 mg of BP crystals were added into 30.00 mL of NMP (containing saturated NaOH) solution, and sonicated in an ice-bath for 11 h. Then, the obtained brown suspension was centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 5 min to remove the residual unexfoliated particles, and the supernatant was collected for use. Prior to use, a certain amount of supernatant was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min, washed twice and then dispersed in DDW to give a brown solution of BPNPs with concentration of 0.17 mg mL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD based chiral sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Bare GCE was polished to yield a mirror-like surface by 0.05 μm alumina slurry, and completely cleaned in DDW by ultrasonication, followed by dried under nitrogen (N2) flow. The as-bought G2-β-CD was dissolved in DDW to obtain a homogeneous solution (1.0 mg mL−1). The as-bought NF was diluted to 0.5 wt% NF with ethanol. To 4 mL of BPNPs suspension (0.17 mg mL−1) was added 4 mL of G2-β-CD (1.00 mg mL−1), which was homogeneously mixed to obtain a BPNSs-G2-β-CD suspension. BPNSs-G2-β-CD suspension (8 μL) was directly pipetted onto the polished GCE and dried with infrared radiation heating to obtain BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE. Then 3 μL of NF was used as the protective film to immobilize BPNSs-G2-β-CD on the electrode surface to obtain NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE.

G2-β-CD (8 μL) and BPNSs (8 μL) modified electrodes (G2-β-CD/GCE and BPNSs/GCE) were prepared in the same manner as BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE, respectively. NF (3 μL) modified electrodes (NF/GCE) were also fabricated for comparison experiments.

The morphologies of as-prepared BPNSs were investigated by HR-TEM. As shown in Fig. 1A and B, the two typical HR-TEM images indicated that few-layered BPNSs with high crystalline quality were prepared [74].

FE-SEM characterization of BPNSs, BPNSs-G2-β-CD and NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD films on electrode was carried out. It can be seen from Fig. 1C that BPNSs on the electrode substrate show a non-uniform and discontinuous flake-like structure, which is transparent and considerably thin with smaller particle sizes. As can be seen from Fig. 1D, BPNSs-G2-β-CD film has a fuzzy, thickened and stacked flake-like structure, revealing that G2-β-CD was cross-linked with BPNSs, which was beneficial to the effective immobilization of the film and resulted in useful synergistic effects [78]. The NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD film exhibits a thick flake-like structure (Fig. 1E), which is attributed to the fact that NF as a stabilizer with high ion-exchange capacity could effectively immobilize BPNSs-G2-β-CD on the electrode surface but not inhibit electron transfer. FE-SEM images of G2-β-CD and BPNSs-G2-β-CD composite are shown in Fig. 1F and Fig. 1G, respectively. Obviously, G2-β-CD shows a rod-like and rough network structure. BPNSs-G2-β-CD composite exhibits a stacked, fractured and rough pore structure, which might be attributed to the synergistic effects of BPNSs and G2-β-CD. Subsequently, the surface composition and elemental mapping of BPNSs-G2-β-CD composite were studied by EDS (Fig. 1H-L). It can be observed from SEM image of BPNSs-G2-β-CD (Fig. 1H) and its corresponding elemental mappings of carbon (C, Fig. 1I), oxygen (O, Fig. 1J) and phosphorus (P, Fig. 1K) that G2-β-CD was successfully compounded with BPNSs. Furthermore, EDS spectrum of BPNSs-G2-β-CD (Fig. 1L) confirms the C, O and P composition of BPNSs-G2-β-CD composite, which is consistent with the above results.

The electrochemical properties of modified electrodes were characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in a 0.1 M KCl using 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- as the electroactive probe. As shown in Fig. 2A, a characteristic reversible redox process with a peak-to-peak separation in potentials (ΔEp) of 140 mV for bare GCE could be observed, whereas the peak currents gradually increased at BPNSs/GCE, G2-β-CD/GCE and BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE, and the ΔEp were 108 mV, 95 mV and 86 mV, respectively. The results indicated that BPNSs, G2-β-CD and BPNSs-G2-β-CD could act as conductive bridges, promote electron transfer between the electrodes and the analytes. Furthermore, BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE exhibited the highest electrochemical activity and reversibility, indicating that BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE possessed better electrocatalytic activity due to the synergistic effects of BPNSs and G2-β-CD. When bare GCE was modified with NF film, no reversible redox process could be observed, indicating that NF exhibited no catalytic activity [76]. Compared to NF/GCE, NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE showed an increased redox peak current, which could be attributed to a deeper level of synergistic effect of BPNSs, NF and G2-β-CD [79].

The electrocatalytic activity of modified electrodes was further investigated by EIS. Usually, the semicircle diameter in Nyquist plots of the EIS is equal to the value of electron-transfer resistance (Rct). Compared with bare GCE (103.2 Ω), BPNSs-G2-β-CD, G2-β-CD and BPNSs modified electrodes exhibited lower Rct of 92.74 Ω, 95.3 Ω and 101.9 Ω, respectively, which can be ascribed to the excellent electrocatalytic activity of BPNSs and G2-β-CD (Fig. 2B). Obviously, the lowest Rct of BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE might be due to the higher electrocatalytic activity and the synergistic effects of BPNSs and G2-β-CD. In addition, the Rct of NF/GCE was increased to 119.9 Ω, again confirming the lower catalytic activity of NF. However, the Rct of NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE was decreased to 114.2 Ω, suggesting that the high catalytic activity of BPNSs-G2-β-CD and the synergistic effects of BPNSs, NF and G2-β-CD both contributed to the electrocatalytic activity of modified electrode, which were consistent with the results of CVs.

The electrochemical chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers was performed by immersion of various modified electrodes (bare GCE, BPNSs/GCE, G2-β-CD/GCE, BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE and NF/BPNPs-G2-β-CD/GCE) into aqueous solutions of 1.0 mM of D-Trp or L-Trp (containing 0.1 M of PB, pH 7.0), where the electrochemical responses of Trp enantiomers were recorded by SWV. As shown in Fig.3A, the SWV peak potential and peak current response of L-Trp and D-Trp at bare GCE were almost overlapped, suggesting that bare GCE has almost no ability for chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers due to the lack of chiral surface. G2-β-CD/GCE (peak current response ratio of L-Trp to D-Trp, IL/ID = 1.16; peak potential difference, ΔEp = ED-EL = 12 mV, Fig. 3C) showed higher chiral recognition efficiency than BPNSs/GCE (IL/ID = 1.03, ΔEp = 4 mV, Fig. 3B), which might be attributed to the chiral nature including the hydrophobic cavity and the hydroxyl (-OH) groups of G2-β-CD that could be selectively interact with Trp enantiomers. IL/ID was increased to 1.18 at BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE (Fig. 3D), indicating that there were synergistic effects between BPNSs and G2-β-CD. Furthermore, NF/BPNPs-G2-β-CD/GCE exhibited the highest chiral recognition efficiency (IL/ID = 1.49, ΔEp = 20 mV) after immobilization of NF (Fig. 3E), which was attributed to the fact that the emerged synergistic effects of NF and BPNSs-G2-β-CD, as well as the effective retention of water soluble G2-β-CD on the electrode for inhibiting its dissolution erosion [55].

Three-point interaction model is recognized as a tool for proposing chiral recognition mechanism. The key step in the three-point interaction model is the formation of diastereomers between the chiral selector and the enantiomers [80]. Specially, the possible chiral recognition in this work is due to the formation of new diastereomers between D- or L-Trp enantiomers and NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD, which would cause Gibbs free energy differences [81]. It is worth mentioning that the peak current response of L-Trp was much larger than that of compared D-Trp at NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE, suggesting that the developed chiral sensor preferentially interacted with L-Trp rather than D-Trp [80]. Maybe the fact is that when the phenyl group of Trp extends into the cavity of G2-β-CD, the branched chiral –OH groups of NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD and the chiral -NH2 group of L-Trp could selectively form hydrogen bonds due to the more suitable intermolecular distances [55]. Therefore, L-Trp could be easily enriched at the surface of the chiral sensing platform, and the peak current response was enhanced. In addition, the peak potential of L-Trp at NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE shifted more negatively than that of D-Trp. In short, the chiral cavity and –OH groups of G2-β-CD, as well as the synergic effect of NF and BPNSs-G2-β-CD made great contributions to the effective chiral recognition (Scheme 2 ).

In order to improve the electrochemical chiral recognition efficiency of the developed chiral sensor for Trp enantiomers, the effects of some important parameters including buffer electrolytes, pH and temperature were investigated in detail.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of types of buffer electrolyte and pH value
ce:section.ce:para: Effects of BR, PB, PBS and HAc-NaAc in the chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers (1.0 mM) at NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE were studied. As shown in Fig. 4A, the maximum recognition efficiency (I L /I D = 1.49, ΔEp = 20 mV) can be reached in PB solution. Therefore, PB was used as a buffer electrolyte throughout the following experiments. Furthermore, to evaluate the effects of pH, the Trp enantiomers solutions were prepared using PB solution with different pH values. It can be observed from Fig. 4B that the chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers significantly changed in the pH range 5.0–9.0, and the highest recognition efficiency could be obtained at pH 7.0, suggesting that both acidic and alkaline conditions are unfavorable for the identification of Trp enantiomers. In addition, it can be clearly seen in Fig. 4C that as the pH value increased, the oxidation peak potentials of L-Trp and D-Trp at NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE were negatively shifted, again indicating that protons were directly involved in the electrochemical oxidation process [82]. Simultaneously, the slope value was close to the theoretical value (59 mV/pH), as can be seen from the regression equations for potential and pH (Fig. 4C), confirming the existence of equal proton-electron transfer during electrochemical oxidation process [83].

In fact, temperature plays a key role in both intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions [84]. The chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers at NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE was investigated in temperature range of 10–40 °C ( Fig. 4D). IL/ID and ΔEp gradually increased with an increase of temperature from 10 to 25 °C and reached a maximum value at 25 °C, then decreased under higher temperature. The interference of the hydroxyl groups of water which were participating in hydrogen-bonding with both chiral electrode interface and chiral amino acid should be eliminated, and chiral recognition could be achieved only when the differences in intermolecular forces of water molecules and NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD, and Trp enantiomers and NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD were obvious [85]. The chiral –OH groups of NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD and the chiral -NH2 groups of L-Trp could selectively form stereospecific hydrogen-bonding, as well as the binding interaction between the aromatic ring of L-Trp and the chiral hydrophobic cavity of NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD. It's worth noting that the chiral‑hydrogen-bonding-controlled intermolecular cross-linking and the chiral-hydrophobic-cavity-triggered embedding effect are stereospecific and stable at 25 °C [86]. At low temperature, the hydrogen-bonding between NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD and L-Trp were suppressed by the internal water molecules, resulting in reduced recognition efficiency. With an increase of temperature, the interaction of water molecules within NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD would decrease, while the hydrogen-bonding interactions between L-Trp and NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD were enhanced, leading to increased recognition efficiency [85]. Furthermore, the intermolecular hydrogen-bonding formed between NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD and L-Trp would be weakened at higher temperatures, and the chiral recognition efficiency significantly decreased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical chiral detection of Trp enantiomers
ce:section.ce:para:
Under optimized experimental conditions described above, the electrochemical sensing properties of NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE toward L-Trp and D-Trp in the concentration range 0.01–1.0 mM were investigated by SWV (Fig. 5 , inset a, b). It can be seen from Fig. 5 that the relationships between the peak currents and the concentrations of L-Trp and D-Trp were linear. A linear regression equation of L-Trp can be expressed as ip (μA) = 21.09 CL-Trp + 0.18 (R
2 = 0.99634) with a detection limit of 1.07 μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (S/N = 3), as well as that of D-Trp was expressed as ip (μA) = 13.25 CD-Trp + 0.45 (R
2 = 0.99121) with a detection limit of 1.71 μM (S/N = 3). Furthermore, a comparison of the developed sensor with previously reported chiral sensors is listed (Table 1 ). The Hummers' method for preparation of GO was involved in fabricating 3D-G/HP-β-CD/GCE, rGO/γ-CD/GCE and β-CD-PtNPs/GNs/GCE, therefore, the relatively tedious preparation procedures were time-consuming. Simultaneously, the electrodeposition methods for fabricating SCS/GCE and Cu2-β-CD/GCE, a combination of electrodeposition and impregnation for preparing PS/P-L-Glu/GCE, a combination of autopolymerization and stirring involved in building MWCNT@PDA/Cu(II)/GCE, and an amidation process for constructing CMC-CS/GCE, as well as the diamine functionalization involved in preparing MWCNTs-EA-NCCS/GCE were also relatively complex and time-consuming. Obviously, the developed NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE possessed many advantages such as easy-to-fabricate, facile and rapid. It's worth mentioning that NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE possessed lower detection limit than many previously reported sensors such as rGO/γ-CD/GCE, CMC-CS/GCE and β-CD-PtNPs/GNs/GCE, indicating it had relatively higher sensitivity. In addition, NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE possessed relatively bigger ΔEp compared with those of SCS/GCE, MWCNT@PDA/Cu(II)/GCE, MWCNTs-EA-NCCS/GCE, β-CD-PtNPs/GNs/GCE and Cu2-β-CD/GCE, making it be a promising electrochemical chiral sensor.

The chiral recognition specificity of NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE for the enantiomers of tyrosine (Tyr), Trp, histidine (His) and phenylalanine (Phe) was investigated. It can be observed that the NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE shows stereospecificity for Trp enantiomers due to the host-guest size matching principle between G2-β-CD and amino acid enantiomers (Fig. 6A) [87]. The interactions between the chiral hydrophobic cavity of G2-β-CD and the aromatic groups of the abovementioned amino acids dominated the chiral recognition specificity (Fig. 6B) [54]. The mono-aromatic rings of Phe and the heterocyclic ring of His could not penetrate into the cavity of G2-β-CD, and no significant electrochemical response toward Phe and His at NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE could be observed. In sharp contrast, NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD exhibited lower recognition efficiency for Tyr which possesses a p-hydroxyphenyl group due to the relatively weak intermolecular hydrogen bonding interaction between NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD and Tyr [51].

The highest chiral recognition efficiency of NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE for Trp enantiomers could be found. Trp possesses one chiral side-chain, an indolyl group, which is a polycyclic aromatic ring that can penetrate into the chiral cavity of G2-β-CD. The two intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions, one formed between the chiral –OH group of G2-β-CD and the chiral -NH2 group of Trp, the other formed between the chiral –OH group of G2-β-CD and the -NH- of indolyl group of Trp, as well as the hydrophobic-cavity-triggered embedding effect due to the appropriate size of the hydrophobic cavity and the indolyl group, dominated the effective chiral recognition [48–50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference, reproducibility and stability of NF/BPNS-G2-β-CD/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Recognition selectivity and stability are important parameters for a newly developed chiral sensor. The possible interferences of physiological substances and some mineral salts in 0.1 mM of Trp enantiomers at NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE were investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 , the 10-fold higher concentrations of interfering substances (such as ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glc), l-serine (L-Ser), L-phenylalanine (L-Phe), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), copper chloride (CuCl2), cmagnesium nitrate [Mg(NO3)2], aluminum nitrate [Al(NO3)3] and zinc nitrate [Zn(NO3)2]) produced no significant changes for the detection of Trp enantiomers, and a decrease of peak currents by less than 3.38% relative to the initial levels could be observed, indicating that the proposed NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE possessed high selectivity and excellent anti-interference ability for the detection of Trp enantiomers.

The reproducibility of NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE in 0.1 mM Trp enantiomers was estimated for six independent times, and relative standard deviation (RSD) of 4.06% for L-Trp and 3.38% for D-Trp can be obtained, respectively. The stability of NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE in 0.1 mM of Trp enantiomers was also investigated for six successive times by SWV, and RSD of L-Trp and D-Trp was calculated to be 2.14% and 3.12%, respectively. Therefore, the proposed chiral sensor, NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE, exhibited good reproducibility and stability for the analysis of Trp enantiomers.

In summary, a novel chiral sensor based on NF stabilized BPNSs-G2-β-CD composite modified GCE was firstly fabricated, which could be used to recognize Trp enantiomers. NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE showed excellent recognition ability (IL/ID = 1.49, ΔEp = ED - EL = 20 mV) for Trp enantiomers. We proposed that the intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions as well as the hydrophobic-cavity-triggered embedding effect dominated the effective chiral recognition. In addition, the fabricated NF/BPNSs-G2-β-CD/GCE showed relatively high stability, high resistance to interference and good repeatability as well as excellent reproducibility. The proposed chiral sensor would provide some useful references for constructing novel chiral platforms in the future.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.
Jiao Zou: Data curation, Software, Validation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Jin-Gang Yu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.
